Capstone Project - Walmart Dataset¶

In [233]:
# Importing basic Libraries

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score

from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split

import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")
def fxn():
    warnings.warn("deprecated", DeprecationWarning)
with warnings.catch_warnings(action="ignore"):
    fxn()

walmart_df = pd.read_csv(filepath)

In [235]:
walmart_df['Holiday_Flag'].nunique()
Out[235]:
2

Observations

  • We have 45 stores. So to see performance of each store, we may need to take input of store number.
  • It seems there is Temperature data also, indicating climatic conditions may be affecting the sales in some way.
  • Weekly sales are given, there is a consistent span of 7 days of records. Weekly sales must be the amount of sales happened in that week span.
  • CPI is the consumer price index, which indicates a ratio of consumption; sort of inflation factor. Probably indicating high CPI is high prices of Fuel.
  • and parallelly, Uneployment rate may be a trend of records of the municipal.
  • Probably the holiday flag may be that there might have been a holiday or more in the specific week.
In [236]:
# Firstly we get a date index to begin with

walmart_df['Date']= pd.to_datetime(walmart_df['Date'], errors='coerce', format="%d-%m-%Y")  # converts the Date object to datetime format
                                                                                            # included the format parameter to avoid errors moving forward
walmart_df.set_index("Date", inplace=True)     # make the Date column as the index
# walmart_df.sort_index(inplace=True, ascending=True)    # sort as per ascending order

walmart_df.info()

memory usage: 402.2 KB

We see in the .info that all the Dtypes are as needed.

In [237]:
walmart_df.head()

Date
2010-02-05 1 1643690.90 0 42.31 2.572 211.096358 8.106
2010-02-12 1 1641957.44 1 38.51 2.548 211.242170 8.106
2010-02-19 1 1611968.17 0 39.93 2.514 211.289143 8.106
2010-02-26 1 1409727.59 0 46.63 2.561 211.319643 8.106
2010-03-05 1 1554806.68 0 46.50 2.625 211.350143 8.106

1. You are provided with the weekly sales data for their various outlets. Use statistical analysis, EDA, outlier analysis, and handle the missing values to come up with various insights that can give them a clear perspective on the following:

  • a. If the weekly sales are affected by the unemployment rate, if yes - which stores are suffering the most?
  • b. If the weekly sales show a seasonal trend, when and what could be the reason?
  • c. Does temperature affect the weekly sales in any manner?
  • d. How is the Consumer Price index affecting the weekly sales of various stores?
  • e. Top performing stores according to the historical data.
  • f. The worst performing store, and how significant is the difference between the highest and lowest performing stores.

2. Use predictive modeling techniques to forecast the sales for each store for the next 12 weeks.

Here we will solve the Q2 of the given project, i.e. Predictive Model building exercise.¶

In [238]:
# adding a Month number and Year number to the series, if we need it in ARIMAX or SARIMAX factoring

walmart_df['Month']= pd.DatetimeIndex(walmart_df.index).month   # extract the month number of the transaction
walmart_df['Year']= pd.DatetimeIndex(walmart_df.index).year     # extract the year number if the transaction
walmart_df.head()

Since there are 45 stores, we will split the data for different stores. We will take an input from the user for store numbers and then proceed for that selected store.

In [240]:
number_of_stores= int(walmart_df['Store'].nunique())
print(f'Dataset contains complete data from {number_of_stores} stores.')

Dataset contains complete data from 45 stores.
In [241]:
plt.figure(figsize=(13,4.5))
store_sales = walmart_df.groupby('Store', as_index=False)['Weekly_Sales'].sum().sort_values(by='Weekly_Sales', ascending=False)

sns.barplot(
    data=store_sales,
    x='Store',
    y='Weekly_Sales',
    palette='viridis'
)

plt.title('Total Sales by Store', fontsize=16, color='gold', fontweight='semibold')
plt.xlabel('Store', fontsize=12, color='white')
plt.ylabel('Total Weekly Sales', fontsize=12, color='white')
plt.grid(alpha=0.3)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
In [242]:
# Since there are 45 Stores, we will obtain store number from the user

while True:
    try:
        store_number = int(input(f"Enter Store Number (1-{number_of_stores}): "))
        if 1 <= store_number <= number_of_stores:
            break
        else:
            print(f"Invalid input. Please enter a number between 1 and {number_of_stores}.")
    except ValueError:
        print("Invalid input. Please enter a valid integer.")

store= walmart_df[walmart_df.Store == store_number]
print(f'Selected Store Number is {store_number}')

store

For building a predictive model, we may not need all the data. Estimating from the weekly sales might give adequate results. We'll drop rest of the data. We'll keep the Holiday Flag column to see further how much affect it has on the sales.

In [244]:
drop_cols= ['Store', 'Temperature', 'Fuel_Price', 'CPI', 'Unemployment', 'Month', 'Year']
store.drop(drop_cols, axis=1, inplace=True)
new_col_name= 'Weekly_Sales_Store-' + str(store_number)
store.rename(columns={'Weekly_Sales': new_col_name}, inplace=True)
store

Checking for outliers:

In [245]:
sns.boxplot(x=store[new_col_name])
plt.show()

We see outliers.

In [246]:
Q1= store[new_col_name].quantile(0.25)
Q3= store[new_col_name].quantile(0.75)
IQR= Q3-Q1

lower_bound= Q1-(1.5*IQR)
upper_bound= Q3+(1.5*IQR)

outliers= store[(store[new_col_name] < lower_bound) | (store[new_col_name] > upper_bound)]
print(f"Number of Outliers: {outliers.shape[0]}")
print(f"Data shape before cleaning: {store.shape[0]}")

store= store[(store[new_col_name]>=lower_bound) & (store[new_col_name]<=upper_bound)]
print(f"Data shape after cleaning: {store.shape[0]}")

sns.boxplot(x=store[new_col_name])
plt.show()

Number of Outliers: 8
Data shape before cleaning: 143
Data shape after cleaning: 135

We'll run a T-Test on holiday sales vs non-holiday sales

In [249]:
from scipy.stats import ttest_ind

holiday_sales = store[store['Holiday_Flag'] == 1][new_col_name]
non_holiday_sales = store[store['Holiday_Flag'] == 0][new_col_name]

t_stat, p_value = ttest_ind(holiday_sales, non_holiday_sales, equal_var=False)

print('T-test : Holiday vs Non-Holiday Sales\n----------------------------')
print(f'T-Statistic : {t_stat:.4f}')
print(f'P-Value : {p_value:.4e}')

if p_value < 0.05:
    print(' There is significant difference between holiday and non-holiday sales')
else:
    print('There isnt any significant difference between holiday and non-holiday sales')

T-test : Holiday vs Non-Holiday Sales
----------------------------
T-Statistic : 0.4501
P-Value : 6.6512e-01
There isnt any significant difference between holiday and non-holiday sales
In [250]:
from statsmodels.tsa.seasonal import seasonal_decompose

print(f"Seasonal Decomposition plots for Store No. {store_number}")
decomposition = seasonal_decompose(store[new_col_name], period=52)  # 52 weeks = 1 year
fig = plt.figure()  
fig = decomposition.plot()  
fig.set_size_inches(13, 10)
plt.show()

Seasonal Decomposition plots for Store No. 9

<Figure size 640x480 with 0 Axes>

In most of the stores, consistent seasonility can be obeserved. For now, we can assume that all the stores are showing seasonality and we'll preceed further. In case seasonality is not observed in a certain store, please connect with the developer. Changes will be incorporated accorsdingly.

In [251]:
# Check the Plot
# store.plot(figsize= (20,7))
# plt.legend()
# plt.show()

plt.figure(figsize=(13,4.5))
sns.lineplot(data=store, x='Date', y=new_col_name, color='lime', linewidth=1.5)

plt.title('Sales Trends Over Time', fontsize=16, color='gold', fontweight='semibold')
plt.xlabel('Date', fontsize=12)
plt.ylabel('Weekly Sales', fontsize=12)
plt.grid(alpha=0.3)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

In most of the stores, the trend seems to be pretty linear. Though seasonility seems more prominient.
Most of the show that The period between June to August shows a medium-high sales, while between December-January the sales are at peak.
Some stores have a more consistent sales trend with the occassional high, while some show that around April-May, usually it is a lowest sales periods.

In [252]:
# Running AD Fuller test

from statsmodels.tsa.stattools import adfuller
adf_result= adfuller(store[new_col_name])

# Display results
print("  AD-Fuller Test Results\n  -----------------------")
print("ADF Statistic:", adf_result[0])
print("p-value:", adf_result[1])
print("Critical Values:", adf_result[4])

# Hypothesis testing
print("\n\n Hypothesis Testing\n -------------------")
if(adf_result[1]>0.05):
    print(f"The data for store No. {store_number} is not stationary")
else:
    print(f"The data for store No. {store_number} is stationary")

  AD-Fuller Test Results
  -----------------------
ADF Statistic: -1.3862562424320986
p-value: 0.5888470256725093
Critical Values: {'1%': np.float64(-3.4851223522012855), '5%': np.float64(-2.88553750045158), '10%': np.float64(-2.5795685622144586)}


 Hypothesis Testing
 -------------------
The data for store No. 9 is not stationary

Most of the data for most of the stores is observed to be stationary.

Model Building¶

ARIMA - p, d, q

  • ar Auto Regression (past values) - p
  • i differencing order / values - d
  • ma Moving Averages (past errors) - q
In [253]:
# Trying to get an estimate of P & q from a graph

from statsmodels.graphics.tsaplots import plot_acf, plot_pacf

fig, axes= plt.subplots(1,2, figsize= (10,5))

plot_acf(store[new_col_name], ax=axes[0], lags=7)
axes[0].set_title("ACF")

plot_pacf(store[new_col_name], ax=axes[1], lags=7)
axes[1].set_title("PACF")

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
In [254]:
# # For Running Iter tools, we split the data to train-test

train_size= int(len(store)*0.85)  # 85% is considered as the training size

train_df= store[new_col_name][:train_size]
test_df= store[new_col_name][train_size:]
In [255]:
# taking last 52 weeks (1 year) as the test size, and all others as the training data

# train_df= store[:-52]
# test_df= store[-52:]

This train-test split was included later after the 80-20 split test runs because it's giving better predictions. Hence we will keep using this split.

In [256]:
import itertools
from statsmodels.tsa.arima.model import ARIMA
from sklearn.metrics import mean_squared_error

p= range(0,10)  # Auto Regression (past values)
d= range(1,2)   # differencing order
q= range(0,9)   # moving average (past errors)

pdq= list(itertools.product(p,d,q))
rmse= []
order1= []

for i in pdq:
    model= ARIMA(train_df, order=i)
    model_fit= model.fit()
    pred= model_fit.predict(start= len(train_df), end= len(train_df)+len(test_df)-1)
    error= np.sqrt(mean_squared_error(test_df, pred))

    order1.append(i)
    rmse.append(error)

result = pd.DataFrame(index=order1, data=rmse, columns=["RMSE score"])
In [257]:
result.sort_values(by='RMSE score', inplace=True)
In [258]:
# fitting the calculated results into the model and observing the performance

best_model= result['RMSE score'].idxmin()
print(f'best factors for ARIMA is calculated at (p, d, q) = {best_model} for store No. {store_number}')
model1= ARIMA(store[new_col_name], order=best_model)
result= model1.fit()
store['ARIMA_Predicted']= result.predict()
store

best factors for ARIMA is calculated at (p, d, q) = (4, 1, 3) for store No. 9

forecast_value= forecast.predicted_mean

SARIMA Model building¶

to use PMDARIMA to automatically fit a Seasonal AutoRegressive Integrated Moving Average with eXogenous variables (SARIMAX) model to the weekly sales data, the parameters are set for automatic selection based on the Akaike Information Criterion (AIC) through the 'auto_arima' function as below:

In [261]:
from pmdarima import auto_arima
In [262]:
# Auto-ARIMA on store data
stepwise_fit= auto_arima(store[new_col_name],
                         seasonal=True,
                         m=52,
                         trace=False,
                         stepwise=True)
print(stepwise_fit.summary())

auto_arima_bestModel_store= stepwise_fit.order
print(f"best fit model: {stepwise_fit.order}")

                                     SARIMAX Results                                      
==========================================================================================
Dep. Variable:                                  y   No. Observations:                  135
Model:             SARIMAX(1, 1, 1)x(0, 0, 1, 52)   Log Likelihood               -1587.574
Date:                            Sat, 13 Dec 2025   AIC                           3183.149
Time:                                    17:20:00   BIC                           3194.740
Sample:                                         0   HQIC                          3187.859
                                            - 135                                         
Covariance Type:                              opg                                         
==============================================================================
                 coef    std err          z      P>|z|      [0.025      0.975]
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
ar.L1          0.6355      0.105      6.065      0.000       0.430       0.841
ma.L1         -0.8049      0.101     -7.951      0.000      -1.003      -0.606
ma.S.L52      -0.1597      0.108     -1.479      0.139      -0.371       0.052
sigma2      1.171e+09   2.14e-11   5.47e+19      0.000    1.17e+09    1.17e+09
===================================================================================
Ljung-Box (L1) (Q):                   2.27   Jarque-Bera (JB):                14.25
Prob(Q):                              0.13   Prob(JB):                         0.00
Heteroskedasticity (H):               0.83   Skew:                            -0.17
Prob(H) (two-sided):                  0.55   Kurtosis:                         4.56
===================================================================================

Warnings:
[1] Covariance matrix calculated using the outer product of gradients (complex-step).
[2] Covariance matrix is singular or near-singular, with condition number 2.28e+35. Standard errors may be unstable.
best fit model: (1, 1, 1)
In [263]:
# auto ARIMA on Training DF

stepwise_fit= auto_arima(train_df,
                         seasonal=True,
                         m=52,
                         trace=False,
                         stepwise=True)
print(stepwise_fit.summary())

auto_arima_bestModel_train= stepwise_fit.order
print(f"best fit model: {stepwise_fit.order}")

In [264]:
# Checking the resul with Best model from Auto-ARIMA for store data

print(f'best factors for ARIMA is calculated at (p, d, q) = {auto_arima_bestModel_store} for store No. {store_number}')
model1= ARIMA(store[new_col_name], order=auto_arima_bestModel_store)
result= model1.fit()
store['autoARIMA_Predicted']= result.predict()

store[[new_col_name, 'autoARIMA_Predicted']].plot(figsize=(20,7))
plt.show()

best factors for ARIMA is calculated at (p, d, q) = (1, 1, 1) for store No. 9
In [265]:
# Checking the resul with Best model from Auto-ARIMA for store data

print(f'best factors for ARIMA is calculated at (p, d, q) = {auto_arima_bestModel_train} for store No. {store_number}')
model1= ARIMA(store[new_col_name], order=auto_arima_bestModel_train)
result= model1.fit()
store['autoARIMA_Predicted']= result.predict()

store[[new_col_name, 'autoARIMA_Predicted']].plot(figsize=(20,7))
plt.show()

best factors for ARIMA is calculated at (p, d, q) = (1, 1, 1) for store No. 9
In [266]:
from statsmodels.tsa.statespace.sarimax import SARIMAX

sarima_model= SARIMAX(store[new_col_name], order=auto_arima_bestModel_store, seasonal_order=(1, 1, 1, 52))
sarima_result = sarima_model.fit(disp=False)

print(sarima_result.summary())

                                     SARIMAX Results

Sample:                                         0   HQIC                          1993.724
                                            - 135

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
ar.L1          0.7106      0.410      1.732      0.083      -0.094       1.515
ma.L1         -0.8060      0.355     -2.270      0.023      -1.502      -0.110
ar.S.L52       0.1746      0.252      0.693      0.488      -0.319       0.668
ma.S.L52      -0.6459      0.506     -1.277      0.201      -1.637       0.345
sigma2      2.323e+09   4.66e-10   4.98e+18      0.000    2.32e+09    2.32e+09

Prob(Q):                              0.60   Prob(JB):                         0.23

Warnings:
[1] Covariance matrix calculated using the outer product of gradients (complex-step).
[2] Covariance matrix is singular or near-singular, with condition number 1.97e+34. Standard errors may be unstable.
In [267]:
# Forecast next 12 weeks
forecast_steps = 12
forecast = sarima_result.get_forecast(steps=forecast_steps)

forecast_mean = forecast.predicted_mean
forecast_ci = forecast.conf_int()

forecast_index = pd.date_range(start=store.index[-1] + pd.Timedelta(weeks=1), periods=forecast_steps, freq='W')
In [268]:
plt.figure(figsize=(14,4.5))
plt.plot(store[new_col_name], color='royalblue', label='Past Weekly Sales')
plt.plot(forecast_index, forecast_mean, color='orange', label='Forecast')
plt.fill_between(forecast_index, forecast_ci.iloc[:,0], forecast_ci.iloc[:,1], color='orange', alpha=0.2)
plt.title(f'SARIMA Forecast for Store {store_number} (Next 12 Weeks)', color='gold')
plt.xlabel('Date')
plt.ylabel('Weekly Sales')
plt.legend()
plt.grid(True, linestyle='--', alpha=0.5)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Conclusion¶

We can see above that ARIMA & SARIMA both give a satisfactory result and predictions using either are pretty much the same. Manual best_fit_model as well as auto-ARIMA work equally well in making the prediction in this given dataset.

In [ ]:
